feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: Submission to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act by Scale Ireland
path: Submission to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act by Scale Ireland

paragraphs.text: ScaleIreland

section_path: Submission to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act by Scale Ireland
paragraphs.text: To whom it may concern,

section_path: Submission to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act by Scale Ireland
paragraphs.text: We welcome the opportunity to contribute to this public consultation on the proposed Digital Fairness Act (DFA) on behalf of Scale Ireland, a body representing tech start-ups and scaling companies in Ireland. Whilst we fully support the Commission's underlying objectives, we wish to outline our concerns regarding the introduction of an additional layer of consumer regulation and to suggest alternative measures that would achieve the same policy goals with less burden on emerging businesses.

Scale Ireland and its members share the Commission's aim of protecting consumers from exploitative online practices such as dark patterns and addictive design. Nevertheless, the proposed DFA risks generating unintended harm for start-ups and scaling companies already operating in an exceptionally dense regulatory environment.

Businesses offering online or digital consumer goods and services in the EU are currently subject to very broad reaching national and EU-wide legislation including the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Digital Service Act (DSA), Artificial Intelligence Act (AI), Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) and the Digital Markets Act (DMA). Compliance with this complex regulatory framework is difficult for small and scaling businesses as they need to determine the differing rules that apply to them and how to address these.

Many of these existing pieces of legislation already address the concerns which the proposed DFA will aim to tackle. For example, the UPCD addresses unfair and aggressive commercial practices which could include dark patterns. The GDPR also addresses the use of personal data for targeted and personalised advertising. Start-ups depend on data-driven marketing to reach new markets and grow, and safeguarding their ability to use targeted advertising is essential to maintaining a level playing field. The legal framework is already in place to deal with these concerns and an additional piece of legislation will add to the complexity of compliance with this. The introduction of the DFA would add another layer to this already complex framework and we feel it will prove challenging for businesses to effectively address the DFA alongside their existing regulatory obligations. We feel that the current regulatory framework requires simplification rather than a further regulation to better to allow businesses to better understand and fully meet their obligations under EU and national law.
In addition, the questionnaire issued by the Commission as part of the public consultation provides a breakdown of the practices which the proposed DFA would address.

As referred to in paragraph 1.1 there will be complications for start-ups and scaling businesses in meeting the additional regulatory obligations imposed by the proposed DFA. We think it is important to highlight the financial burden which complex regulation imposes on businesses. There are no robust financial supports in place to provide growing and scaling businesses with the support they
need to meet these regulatory demands and implement the required technological solutions to this regulation.

Unlike large social media companies or retail platforms, start-ups and scaling businesses cannot afford to absorb the costs of regulatory compliance, specialist advice or the potential fines for breaches of regulations. We feel that if the DFA applies in the same manner to all businesses regardless of size it will hinder the growth of start-ups and scaling companies in the EU.

At a time when the European regime is focused on developing the EU as a global centre of innovation and technology it seems counterproductive to introduce additional regulation which may potentially limit the ability of start-ups and scaling businesses to grow and place economic burdens on them. Rather than further regulation and legislation, a consolidation of existing legislation in the form of a one-stop-shop for businesses to learn and understand existing laws and how these can be enforced would be beneficial.

Ireland offers a unique commercial and legal environment which encourages growth and expansion for start-ups and scaling businesses. This is in part due to the strong relationship between the UK and the US, with Ireland acting as a bridge between these countries and the EU. Additionally, Ireland has a strong state agency which invests and supports our businesses which is not seen anywhere else in Europe. Ireland is also the European headquarters of various large social media and other online platforms which means the Irish regulators overseeing the implementation and enforcement of EU law are among the most active in the EU. Ireland also has a unique legislative and political relationship with the UK, a key trading partner of the EU. These nuances apply to Ireland as other unique cultural and societal differences apply to other Member States.

Whilst it remains our submission that the DFA may not be required, we feel that, in light of the nuances outlined above, if the DFA is to be introduced it should take the form of a directive rather than a regulation to allow for a greater degree of flexibility by Member States in the implementation of this legislation. If the DFA is introduced as a regulation there will be no scope for the nuances of Irish law to be reflected by the Irish government in bringing this into Irish law. The introduction of the DFA as a directive would also ensure that national regulators, who have the context and nuance of operating within specific Member States, can play a central role in the enforcement of the DFA as they do with other pieces of consumer legislation.

As we have set out in the first part of this submission the introduction of the DFA poses potential issues for start-ups and scaling businesses across the EU. In this part of the submission, we will set out what we feel are suitable alternatives to the introduction of the proposed DFA.

Given there are already broad protections in place for consumers we feel that the Commission should focus on developing robust, proportionate enforcement practices at a national and Union level for this existing legislation rather than implementing the DFA. We would recommend proportionate enforcement measures for small and scaling businesses be introduced to provide fair enforcement measures for businesses with smaller resources. The European AI Act contains specific provisions allowing for proportionate fines for SMEs and this approach to proportionate enforcement should be reflected in any new enforcement measures introduced.

The Competition and Consumer Protection Commission (CCPC) in Ireland is currently investigating both Temu and Shein for breaches of consumer protection laws including fake discounts, misleading information and fake pressure. These investigations are being run in collaboration with the equivalent authorities in Germany and Belgium and are being completed through the European Commission's

Consumer Protection Cooperation Network. These investigations show the appetite among regulators to investigate some of the practices which the DFA will seek to address within the existing regulatory framework. We feel that a focus on encouraging investigations and potential enforcement of this kind would be a suitable alternative to the introduction of the DFA.

The 2024 Draghi report calls for a simplification of EU regulation to develop competitiveness and competition. This report specifically suggests that the Commission start lowering the "stock" of regulation and better assess the impact which new regulation may have on SMEs and small mid-cap businesses. With this report in mind, we feel that the Commission should focus on the simplification of existing legislation and not move towards the introduction of further regulation such as the DFA. This simplification could take the form of the previously mentioned consolidation of existing legislation to highlight the existing laws that the DFA aims to address. The introduction of the DFA as additional regulation following a raft of newly introduced regulation within the last number of years may be seen as hindering Europe's competitiveness as it is harder for businesses to understand and ensure accurate compliance.

Given the volume of regulation for businesses within the EU (as set out in the examples listed in paragraph 1.1 of this submission) we feel that the Commission should focus on a consolidation and simplification exercise to ensure there is no overlap across existing regulation as opposed to introducing the DFA. The simplification exercise suggested in the Draghi report is something we feel should be prioritised ahead of the introduction of the DFA and would support a pro-European approach to regulation.

We feel that greater clarity on the existing regulations in place would offer a suitable alternative to the DFA. There is a lack of guidance at both an EU and national level as to how businesses (particularly small and scaling businesses) can meet their regulatory obligations and exact requirements under these regulations. We call on the Commission to implement a more robust guidance regime in lieu of additional regulation. This could be done at both a national and EU level and would provide greater clarity to all businesses within the EU.

We would further suggest that sector specific guidance be issued on existing legislation and how this applies to entities operating in this space. This guidance would go some way in addressing the problematic practices which we understand the proposed DFA is intended to address. An example of this would be guidance on influencer marketing and the use of artificial intelligence as is detailed in the questionnaire issued by the Commission as part of the public consultation. Another example follows the recently introduced AI Act with the obligation of Member States to establish or designate independent national competent authorities to oversee sector-compliance with the AI Act. A similar approach could be taken to spread the responsibility of monitoring compliance with existing legislation.

For the reasons set out above, Scale Ireland respectfully submits that the Commission's policy objectives of protecting consumers, fostering trust in digital markets and promoting the competitiveness of European industry would be better served by a renewed commitment to the effective enforcement, clarification and simplification of the existing regulatory framework rather than by the adoption of the DFA as an additional regulation. Should the Commission nonetheless pursue the introduction of the DFA, we urge that any proposal be structured as a directive affording Member States the flexibility required to reflect national legal and commercial realities and that it incorporate proportionate exemptions, phased obligations or tailored compliance pathways for small and scaling enterprises. Regardless of the chosen approach Scale Ireland will work with its members to ensure
